coredata.dc:title: Effects of graphene oxide and graphene quantum dots on enhancing CPP-ACP anti-caries ability of enamel lesion in a biofilm-challenged environment
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Cheng
Zhang, Yu Yuan
Peng, Si-min
Gu, Min
Wong, Hai Ming
coredata.dc:description:
Objective To investigate the anticaries effects of graphene oxide (GO) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) combined with casein phosphopeptide-amorphous calcium phosphate (CPP-ACP) on enamel in a biofilm-challenged environment.
Material and methods GO and GQDs were synthesised using citric acid. The antibiofilm and biofilm inhibition effects for Streptococcus mutans were evaluated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM), and colony-forming units (CFU). Remineralisation ability was determined by assessing mineral loss, calcium-to-phosphorus ratio, and surface morphology. To create a biofilm-challenged environment, enamel blocks were immersed in S. mutans to create the lesion and then subjected to artificial saliva/biofilm cycling for 7 days. Anticaries effects of GO, GQDs, GQDs@CPP-ACP, GO@CPP-ACP, and CPP-ACP were determined by broth pH and mineral changes after 7-day pH cycling. Biocompatibility was tested using a Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK8) assay for human gingival fibroblasts (HGF-1).
Results GQDs and GO presented significant antibiofilm and biofilm inhibition effects compared to the CPP-ACP and control groups (P < 0.05). The enamel covered by GQDs and GO showed better crystal structure formation and less mineral loss (P < 0.05) than that covered by CPP-ACP alone. After 7 days in the biofilm-challenged environment, the GO@CPP-ACP group showed less lesion depth than the CPP-ACP and control groups (P < 0.05). GO and GQDs showed good biocompatibility compared to the control group by CCK8 (P > 0.05) within 3 days.
Conclusion GO and GQDs could improve the anti-caries effects of CPP-ACP, and CPP-ACP agents with GO or GQDs could be a potential option for enamel lesion management. Clinical Significance: GO and GQDs have demonstrated the potential to significantly enhance the anticaries effects of CPP-ACP. Incorporating these nanomaterials into CPP-ACP formulations could provide innovative and effective options for the management of enamel lesions, offering improved preventive and therapeutic strategies in dental care.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Caries
Enamel remineralization
Biofilm
Graphene oxide
CPP-ACP

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of GO, GQDs, and CPP-ACP
Effect of the GO and GQDs on antibiofilm and biofilm inhibition ability
Determination of minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) and minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC)
Remineralization effect of the GO and GQDs on hard tissue combined with CPP-ACP
Artificial saliva/biofilm cycling in a biofilm-challenged environment
Antibiofouling effect of GO and GQDs
Morphologic and structural characterization of biomimetic remineralized enamel
Artificial saliva/biofilm cycling in a biofilm-challenged environment

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by a grant from the Research Grants Council of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region, China (Project No. 17102921). The funding recipient was Hai Ming Wong.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Experimental process. BHIS, brain-heart infusion with 2 % sucrose.

Characterization of CA, GO and GQDs. A. Elemental analysis result of CA, the obtained GQDs and GO. B. FTIR spectra of CA, GQDs and GO. GO, graphene oxide; GQDs, graphene quantum dots; CA, citric acid; FTIR, Fourier-transform infrared.

A. The surviving rate of GO and GQDs at different dilution rates B. The MIC and MBC concentration of GO and GQDs C. The log (CFU/mL) of biofilm in the 6 groups (mean ± SD, n = 3). D. Live/dead bacterial staining images. The green fluorescence represents the live bacteria, and the red indicates the dead bacteria. GO, graphene oxide; GQD, graphene quantum dots; MIC, minimum inhibitory concentration; MBC, minimum bactericidal concentration; CA, citric acid.

A. The log (CFU/mL) of biofilm in the 6 groups (mean ± SD, n = 3). B. Live/dead bacterial staining images. The green fluorescence represented the live bacteria, and the red one indicated the dead. C. Biofilm inhibition effects after 24 h by SEM. CFU, colony-forming unit; GO, graphene oxide; GQDs, graphene quantum dots; MIC, minimum inhibitory concentration; CPP-ACP, casein phosphopeptide-amorphous calcium phosphate.

A. 1-day remineralization by SEM. B. 3-day remineralization by SEM. C. Surface after 1 day of remineralization observed by SEM (10,000× magnification). D. Surface after 3 days of remineralization observed by SEM (10,000× magnification E. Ca/P ratio by EDX after 24 h. F. Mineral gain from remineralization according to ICP-OES. G. Mineral loss from demineralization according to ICP-OES. H. Ca/P ratio before treatment. I. Surface difference of GO@CPP-ACP before and after remineralisation by SEM. SEM, scanning electron microscopy; EDX, energy dispersive X-ray; ICP-OES, inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry; GO, graphene oxide; GQDs, graphene quantum dots; CPP-ACP, casein phosphopeptide-amorphous calcium phosphate; De, demineralization part; Re, remineralization part.

A. Micro-CT of artificial caries formation after 5 days. B. SEM of S. mutans biofilm structure after 5 days. C. Lesion depth of enamel after 7 days pH cycling by Micro-CT. D. The pH of BHIS solution after 4-h immersion at 0,1, 3, 5, and 7 days. E. The lesion depth after 7 days. F. Enamel mineral content after 7 days. CT, computed tomography; SEM, scanning electron microscopy; BHIS, brain-heart infusion with 2 % sucrose; GO, graphene oxide; GQDs, graphene quantum dots; CPP-ACP, casein phosphopeptide-amorphous calcium phosphate.
Percentage of surviving rate of HGF-1 by CCK8 of different concentrations at 4 h, 3 days, and 7 days. MIC, minimum inhibitory concentration.

head.ce:title: Effects of graphene oxide and graphene quantum dots on enhancing CPP-ACP anti-caries ability of enamel lesion in a biofilm-challenged environment

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Paediatric Dentistry and Orthodontics, Faculty of Dentistry, The University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong SAR, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Paediatric Dentistry and Orthodontics, Faculty of Dentistry, The University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong SAR, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Paediatric Dentistry and Orthodontics, Faculty of Dentistry, The University of Hong Kong, 2/F The Prince Philip Dental Hospital, 34 Hospital Road, Sai Ying Pun, Hong Kong SAR, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: 2/F The Prince Philip Dental Hospital, 34 Hospital Road, Sai Ying Pun

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To investigate the anticaries effects of graphene oxide (GO) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) combined with casein phosphopeptide-amorphous calcium phosphate (CPP-ACP) on enamel in a biofilm-challenged environment.

GO and GQDs were synthesised using citric acid. The antibiofilm and biofilm inhibition effects for Streptococcus mutans were evaluated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM), and colony-forming units (CFU). Remineralisation ability was determined by assessing mineral loss, calcium-to-phosphorus ratio, and surface morphology. To create a biofilm-challenged environment, enamel blocks were immersed in S. mutans to create the lesion and then subjected to artificial saliva/biofilm cycling for 7 days. Anticaries effects of GO, GQDs, GQDs@CPP-ACP, GO@CPP-ACP, and CPP-ACP were determined by broth pH and mineral changes after 7-day pH cycling. Biocompatibility was tested using a Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK8) assay for human gingival fibroblasts (HGF-1).

GQDs and GO presented significant antibiofilm and biofilm inhibition effects compared to the CPP-ACP and control groups (P < 0.05). The enamel covered by GQDs and GO showed better crystal structure formation and less mineral loss (P < 0.05) than that covered by CPP-ACP alone. After 7 days in the biofilm-challenged environment, the GO@CPP-ACP group showed less lesion depth than the CPP-ACP and control groups (P < 0.05). GO and GQDs showed good biocompatibility compared to the control group by CCK8 (P > 0.05) within 3 days.
GO and GQDs could improve the anti-caries effects of CPP-ACP, and CPP-ACP agents with GO or GQDs could be a potential option for enamel lesion management.

Clinical Significance: GO and GQDs have demonstrated the potential to significantly enhance the anticaries effects of CPP-ACP. Incorporating these nanomaterials into CPP-ACP formulations could provide innovative and effective options for the management of enamel lesions, offering improved preventive and therapeutic strategies in dental care.

ce:section.ce:para: Dental caries is a chronic disease affecting millions of people worldwide. It is a multifactorial disease that promotes the phasic demineralisation of dental hard tissues [1]. Demineralisation of the tooth structure is due to changes in the oral environment caused by microbial biofilms colonising the tooth surface [2]. Bacteria, especially Streptococcus mutans, produce an acidic biofilm environment owing to their inherited metabolic activity, and generate organic acids through carbohydrate metabolism [3]. Acidic conditions increase the proportion of demineralisation and inhibit the remineralisation of tooth structures [4]. If the demineralisation progress is left unmanaged and the saliva does not supply enough bioavailable calcium and phosphate ions for enamel crystal stem remineralisation, caries may be aggravated chronically, leading to the spread of infection from the enamel to dentin and beyond [5]. Overall, the control of cariogenic biofilms and the promotion of tooth remineralisation are essential. Thus, antimicrobial agents with the same effectiveness as remineralisation in reducing bacterial infections are urgently needed.

Casein phosphopeptide-amorphous calcium phosphate (CPP-ACP) is regarded as a strategic intervention for caries control because it binds calcium and phosphate ions. This concomitant retention of mineral ions and peptides derived from CPP–ACP complexes is attributed to non-covalent interactions between specific salivary proteins and peptides from CPP [6]. The short-term remineralisation effect of CPP-ACP has been reported in several in vitro and in vivo studies [7]. Although the remineralisation effect of CPP-ACP has been demonstrated, recent studies have revealed that CPP-ACP products have limited long-term preventive and therapeutic efficacy against caries in the long run [8]. In addition, CPP-ACP products were much more expensive to use than fluoride products, but no better benefit was offered in reducing early carious lesions in long-term trials compared with fluoride products [9]. This could be because S. mutans planktonic growth was not inhibited and the acidic environment failed to be modified by CPP-ACP [10]. Considering the drawbacks associated with CPP-ACP, alternative strategies for improving its anticaries ability should be explored. Therefore, there is an urgent need to advance the efforts of CPP-ACP in combination with a novel biofilm-inhibiting agent to arrest caries.

To increase the anticaries ability of CPP-ACP, additional agents should be employed to enhance its biofilm inhibition ability. Graphene oxide (GO) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs), such as copper coatings [11], zinc oxide-based nanocomposites, and silica-based multivalent nanoparticles [12], have been exploited for their antimicrobial activities. The excellent biofilm inhibition activity of GO nanosheets was first reported by Hu, marking the beginning of the era of GO and GO-based composite materials as biofilm inhibition materials [13]. Studies have demonstrated that GO-based antimicrobial composites act not only on common bacteria but also on drug-resistant bacteria and even fungi [14]. GQDs, which are defined as 1–10 layers of graphene with a size of <30 nm [15], have been shown to be effective biofilm inhibitors by increasing oxidative stress, damaging cell barriers, and causing cell content leakage [16]. The prominent advantages of GQDs include their large surface area, good solubility in various solvents, suitable photoluminescence stability, low cost, simple functionalization, and low toxicity [17]. Despite the biofilm-inhibiting properties of most carbon-based nanomaterials, the effects of GO and GQDs on S. mutans in oral models and in biofilm-challenged environments have not yet been investigated.

Therefore, the purpose of this study was to assess the biofilm inhibition and antibiofilm effects of GO and GQDs on enamel, as well as their ability to enhance the anticaries effect when combined with CPP-ACP in a biofilm-challenged environment. Thus, our study was structured into 4 distinct parts, each with specific objectives. The first part of our study aimed to demonstrate the antibacterial effects of GO and GQDs, while confirming that CPP-ACP does not exhibit antibacterial properties. Therefore, the results and figures in this section focused primarily on the GO and GQDs groups, highlighting their effectiveness against bacterial growth. The second part in our study investigated whether GO could enhance the remineralization effect of CPP-ACP. In this section, the focus was on comparing the GO@CPP-ACP group with the CPP-ACP group to assess any differences in remineralization capabilities. The third part of our study tested the combined anti-caries effect of CPP-ACP with GO or GQDs in a biofilm-challenged environment. Here, we presented results for the GQDs@CPP-ACP and GO@CPP-ACP groups, as well as the control group, to evaluate their effectiveness in preventing caries under these conditions. The biocompatibility of GO and GQDs with human gingival fibroblasts (HGF-1) was evaluated in the fourth part.

We hypothesised that the presence of GO and GQDs would inhibit the growth and adhesion of S. mutans on the enamel surface, and when combined with CPP-ACP, they would arrest demineralisation by S. mutans and promote remineralisation of artificial enamel caries in a microbiological environment model.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GO, GQDs, and CPP-ACP
ce:section.ce:para: A stable suspension of GO and GQDs was prepared by using citric acid (CA, Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). GO and GQD nanoparticles were synthesised as follows: Two grams of CA were heated to a temperature of 200 °C for a duration of 5 min, resulting in the liquidation of the CA. Subsequently, the liquid was further heated for >30 min until it turned orange, indicating the formation of GQDs. The heating process continued, and after approximately 2 h, the orange liquid eventually transformed into a black solid, indicating the formation of GO. The GO and GQD liquid mixture was subsequently diluted in a NaOH solution until the pH reached 7.0 [18]. GO and GQDs were characterised using a Bio-Rad FTIR UMA 500 (Bio-Rad Laboratories, Hercules, CA, USA) and energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) (Hitachi S-4800 FEG Scanning Electron Microscope; Hitachi Ltd., Tokyo, Japan).
The CPP-ACP product used was Tooth Mousse Plus (GC, Tokyo, Japan), which contains active ingredients of 10 % CPP-ACP.

This study was approved by the Institutional Review Board (IRB No. UW22-657), and extracted caries-free human molars were collected. The teeth were stored in 0.5 % thymol at 4 °C. Enamel slices (3 × 3 × 1.5 mm3) were prepared using a water-cooled diamond saw (IsoMet Low Speed saw; Buehler, Lake Bluff, IL, USA). The working side of the enamel slices was then polished with 2500 ppm, and the non-working surfaces were marked with acid-proof nail polish. All the tooth slices were cleaned ultrasonically.

Streptococcus Mutans (ATCC 35668, ATCC, Manassas, VA, USA) was cultured overnight in brain-heart infusion broth (BHI; Difco, Sparks, MD, USA) at 37 °C anaerobically (85 % N2, 5 % CO2, 10 % H2), harvested by centrifugation with 5000 rpm for 10 min, and resuspended in BHI with 2 % sucrose (BHIS) at a concentration of 106 cells/mL using the McFarland spectrophotometric method at 600 nm optical density (OD600nm) before each experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the GO and GQDs on antibiofilm and biofilm inhibition ability
Determination of minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) and minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC)
ce:section.ce:para: MIC and MBC of prepared GO and GQDs were determined by standard 96-well micro broth dilution methods. BHIS was used to dilute working solutions. One hundred microlitres of each agent at the highest concentration added to the first hole. After adding 100 µL BHIS to the other holes, 100 µL of liquid was taken out from the first hole to mix into the second hole, indicating two-fold dilution of the target agent. The same method was used for double dilution of the remaining holes until the last hole. One hundred microlitres of the solution from the last two holes was removed after mixing. One hundred microlitres of S. mutans with a concentration of 106 cells/mL was added to each hole. Control groups were holes with only 100 µL BHIS and 100 µL S. mutans solution. The 96-well plate was incubated at 37 °C anaerobically (85 % N2, 5 % CO2, 10 % H2) for 24 h. The optical density (OD) was monitored by measuring OD600nm in a microplate reader. The MIC was determined by a sudden drop in the growth curve. For MBC determination, after the measurement of growth curves, 10 µL of the treated bacteria suspensions of each group were taken out and dropped on the agar plate to allow the bacteria to grow for 24 h. The MBC was defined as the lowest concentration at which no bacteria were observed on the plate.

The biofilm inhibitory activities of GO and GQDs were evaluated on S. mutans at a concentration of 106 cells/mL in BHIS using the broth microdilution method. Thirty enamel species were immersed into different solutions: GO (2×MIC, n = 5; 4×MIC, n = 5), GQDs (2×MIC, n = 5; 4×MIC, n = 5), CPP-ACP (n = 5), and BHIS (control, n = 5). After incubation for 24 h, 100 µL of bacterial suspension was added to each sample. The plates were incubated for 24 h at 37 °C anaerobically (85 % N2, 5 % CO2, 10 % H2). One sample from each group was randomly chosen for bacterial viability testing by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM; FluoView FV1000, Olympus, Tokyo, Japan) using LIVE/DEAD BacLight Bacterial Viability Kit (L7012, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM; Hitachi S-4800 FEG Scanning Electron Microscope; Hitachi Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) was used to examine the bacterial morphology by randomly choosing one sample from each group. The samples were fixed in 2.5 % glutaraldehyde at 4 °C overnight, dehydrated in an ascending series of alcohol, dried in a desiccator, and sputter-coated with gold before observation. The remaining samples in each group were counted for colony-forming units (CFU). Tooth slides for CFU were placed in 1 mL of BHI solution, and the bacteria were removed by sonication for 30 s after being washed three times with PBS. Diluted solutions were placed on blood agar and the log10 CFU was calculated after 48 h.

Tooth slides (n = 24) were incubated in 100 μL of S. mutans in BHIS at the concentration of 106 cells/mL. After a 24-hour incubation, each tooth slice was washed three times with PBS. The enamel slices were then incubated in new 96-well plates with the following treatments for 24 h: GO (2×MIC, n = 4; 4×MIC, n = 4), GQDs (2×MIC, n = 4; 4×MIC, n = 4), CPP-ACP (n = 4), and BHIS (control, n = 4). A randomly selected tooth slide from each group was stained for fluorescence imaging. The remaining tooth slides were used for CFU counting.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Remineralization effect of the GO and GQDs on hard tissue combined with CPP-ACP
ce:section.ce:para: Enamel blocks (n = 36) were etched with 37 % phosphoric acid for 30 s and rinsed ultrasonically with deionised water for 1 min. The slices were then incubated with different treatments: GO (4×MIC, n = 6), GQDs (4×MIC, n = 6), GO@CPP-ACP (4×MIC, n = 6), GQDs@CPP-ACP (4×MIC, n = 6), CPP-ACP (n = 6), and control (deionized [DI] water, n = 6). After 24 h of treatment, all samples were immersed in 5 ml of remineralisation solution (2.58 mM CaCl2·2H2O, 1.55 mM KH2PO4, 1 mg/L NaF, 180 mM NaCl, 50 mM Tris−HCl, pH=7) for 1 and 3 days. The solution was changed every day. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (Hitachi S-4800 FEG Scanning Electron Microscope; Hitachi Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) was used to analyse the elemental composition of the calcium-to-phosphorus molar ratio on the enamel blocks to determine the solubility of calcium phosphate. SEM was used to examine the morphology of the enamel surfaces. The concentrations of calcium (Ca) and phosphorus (P) minerals before and after immersion were measured using inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES; Spectro Arcos, Kleve, Germany). The loss of Ca/P was expressed as millimoles per square centimetre (mM cm−2). The average values of Ca/P gain were then analysed.

The demineralisation assay was performed by immersing different treated enamel blocks in 10 mL demineralisation solution (2.2 mM CaCl2, 2.2 mM NaH2PO4, 0.05 M acetic acid, pH 4.5) for 5 h at 37 °C. The slices were incubated with different treatments for 24 h before demineralisation: GO (4×MIC, n = 3), GQDs (4×MIC, n = 3), GO@CPP-ACP (4×MIC, n = 3), GQDs@CPP-ACP (4×MIC, n = 3), CPP-ACP (n = 3), and control (DI water, n = 3). The mean values of the Ca/P loss were analysed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Artificial saliva/biofilm cycling in a biofilm-challenged environment
ce:section.ce:para: The sterile enamel slices were placed in 24-well plates containing 1 mL of S. mutans suspension at 106 cells/mL with BHIS, then incubated at 37 °C anaerobically (90 % N2, 5 % CO2, 5 % H2) for 5 days to create a demineralisation lesion. The demineralised lesions that formed beneath the biofilm were confirmed using micro-CT (MicroCT Skyscan 1172, Bruker, USA) after sonication for 5 min to remove the biofilm. The enamel samples were randomly divided into the following test groups: GO (4×MIC, n = 3), GQDs (4×MIC, n = 3), GO@CPP-ACP (4×MIC, n = 3), GQD@CPP-ACP (4×MIC, n = 3), CPP-ACP(n = 3), and control (DI water, n = 3). Each enamel slice was then transferred to a 24-well plate. An artificial saliva solution was prepared (1.5 mM CaCl2, 0.9 mM KH2PO4, 0.13 mM KCl, and 0.02 mM 4-(2-hydroxyethyl-)−1-piperazineethanesulfonic acid, pH=7). Cylindrical membrane filters were used to remove bacteria. Each sample was treated first by the agent, then immersed in 2 mL of fresh artificial saliva for 20 h at 37 °C, followed by 1 mL of S. mutans suspension at 106 cells/mL with BHIS for 4 h at 37 °C anaerobically (90 % N2, 5 % CO2, 5 % H2) each day. This cycle was repeated for 7 days. On each day, the pH of the BHIS broth after 4 h of immersion was measured using an Orion Dual Star, pH/ISE Benchtop (Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). Micro-CT was used to measure the lesion depth and mineral loss in the enamel slices.

HGF-1 (Otwo Biotech Inc., Shen Zhen, China) were used to measure the toxicities of GO and GQDs. GO and GQDs were diluted with medium to concentrations of 2×, 4×, and 8× the MIC. The HGF-1 were incubated at 37 °C and each plate was seeded 100
μ l
of HGF-1 at a concentration of 105 cells/mL. After 24 h, the cells were exposed to GO and GQDs solutions of different concentrations for 4 h, 3 days, and 5 days. The medium was added as the control. After incubation, cell proliferation was evaluated using the cell counting kit-8 assay (CCK-8 assay; Sigma-Aldrich, USA). In each well, the solution was replaced by 100
μ l
of CCK-8 assay and incubated for 2 h. The survival percentage of HGF-1 was computed as (Atreatment − Ablank)/ (Acontrol − Ablank) × 100 %.
The experimental process is shown in Fig. 1 .

All data were analysed using IBM SPSS V20.0 software (IBM Corporation, Armonk, NY, USA). Normal distribution was assessed by the Shapiro–Wilk test (p > 0.05). One-way ANOVA with the Bonferroni test was used to analyse lesion depth, microhardness, Ca/P molar ratio, and CFUs of each group. The level of significance was set at 5 %.

Based on the EDX analysis results presented in Fig. 2 A, GQDs and GO were observed to have a higher carbon content than CA. This indicated that CA underwent some level of carbonisation during pyrolysis. Moreover, the higher carbon content in GO indicated a higher degree of carbonisation than that of the GQDs. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was conducted to further characterise the obtained CA, GQDs and GO (Fig. 2B). The results revealed that CA, GQDs and GO exhibited absorption of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, indicating the presence of COOH groups in both nanomaterials. Additionally, GO showed almost no absorption of CH, suggesting the complete carbonisation of the CA sample. Furthermore, the COH group (stretching vibration at 1200–1020 cm−1) in GO had been transformed into a COC group (stretching vibration at 1250–1050 cm−1). Notably, the FTIR spectra did not exhibit any characteristic absorption bands of aromatic compounds (such as the stretching vibration of CH in aromatic rings around 3000–3100 cm−1 and skeletal vibration of aromatic rings around 1400–1680 cm−1), indicating the absence of aromatic compounds in the obtained GQDs and GO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibiofouling effect of GO and GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate whether GO and GQDs affect the growth of S. mutans, the growth curves of planktonic S. mutans with different concentrations of GO and GQDs are shown in Fig. 3 A. Fig. 3B shows that GO and GQDs could repress the growth of S. mutans cells, and this repression was positively correlated with the concentration. When the concentration of GO reached 0.25 mg/ml and that of the GQDs reached 3.125 mg/ml, the growth capacity of S. mutans was significantly impaired by 80 %. These results demonstrated that GO and GQDs can rapidly arrest planktonic bacterial growth. Based on this finding, double the MIC (2×MIC) and four times the MIC (4×MIC) were chosen for further experiments.

The reduction in the bacterial counts (log10CFUs/mL) of S. mutans for the antibiofilm activity of the six groups is shown in Fig. 3C. The results showed that the bacterial counts in the GO- and GQD-treated groups were significantly lower than those in the CPP-ACP and control groups. There was no significant difference (P > 0.05) between the groups containing GO and GQDs at 2×MIC. GO was found to have significantly better antibiofilm activity than GQDs at 4×MIC (P < 0.05). In order to compare the S. mutans biofilm structure between the groups with different concentrations, CLSM was performed. Fig. 3D shows that it killed the bacteria after 24 h, and GO had a better antibiofilm effect at higher concentrations. The control and CPP-ACP groups showed that the strain formed a tenacious, well-structured biofilm; however, the GO and GQDs groups had fewer exopolysaccharides with the biofilm.

Regarding biofilm inhibition, a significant difference (P < 0.05) was observed among the GO, GQD, and control groups in terms of bacterial counts (Fig. 4 A). Comparing the groups containing different concentrations of GO and GQDs, a higher concentration of GQDs did not result in significantly lower bacterial counts than the lower concentration group (P > 0.05). However, a significant reduction in bacterial counts with GO was observed when compared with the other concentrations (P < 0.05). No significant differences (P > 0.05) were observed between the CPP-ACP and control groups. Fig. 4B shows that GO and GQDs inhibited biofilm formation, and the higher the concentration, the less biofilm formed. The biofilms of S. mutans treated with GO and GQDs did not show normal morphology, and differences in bacterial growth were observed at different concentrations. In contrast, the S. mutans biofilm fully covered the enamel surface in the CPP-ACP and control groups. SEM in Fig. 4C reached the same conclusion. Fewer bacterial cells were observed on the enamel surface in the GO and GQDs groups in a concentration-dependent manner.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphologic and structural characterization of biomimetic remineralized enamel
ce:section.ce:para: The results of the surface morphology assessment by SEM at ×5000 and ×10,000 magnifications are presented in Fig. 5 A–D. The concentrations of GO and GQDs were 1 mg/mL (4×MIC) and 12.5 mg/mL (4×MIC), respectively. A honeycomb view could be seen on the surfaces in the control, GO, and GQDs groups, owing to enamel surface demineralisation (Fig. 5A). In the GO@CPP-ACP, GQDs@CPP-ACP, and CPP-ACP groups, the porous structure of the enamel prisms was covered by a uniform layer similar to the structure of hydroxyapatite crystals after 1 day (Fig. 5C). As shown in Fig. 5B, the structure of the enamel demineralisation surface was completely covered by hydroxyapatite clusters, and the honeycomb view of the enamel prisms was not visible after 3 days. However, the structure of the hydroxyapatite crystals were different, as shown in Fig. 5D. Fig. 5I illustrates the surface differences of the same enamel sample at different time points. The enamel was half covered by nail polish after demineralisation by phosphoric acid, and washed by acetone after remineralization. In the SEM image, the section labelled "De" represents the enamel surface prior to remineralization, while the section labelled "Re" depicts the enamel surface post-remineralization. Although there is a possibility of teeth variation influencing the results, the SEM images suggested that such variation had minimal impact on the enamel structure. This indicated that the observed changes were primarily attributable to the remineralization process rather than inherent differences in tooth structure.

The calcium-to-phosphorus ratios for the six groups, as determined by EDX analysis, are presented in Fig. 5E. Prior to the experiment, the Ca/P ratios were examined (Fig. 5H), revealing no significant differences among the groups. The results shown in Fig. 5E indicate that the Ca/P ratios in the GO- and GQD-treated groups were not significantly higher than those in the control group. However, the CPP-ACP groups (including GO@CPP-ACP and GQDs@CPP-ACP groups) showed significantly higher Ca/P ratios than the control group. After putting in the remineralisation solution for 24 h, ICP-OES calculations showed significant increases in Ca and P in the three groups in which the enamel was coated with CPP-ACP (P < 0.05), as shown in Fig. 5F. No significant differences were observed between the GO, GQDs, and control groups. Mineral loss after 5 h of exposure to the demineralisation solution is shown in Fig. 5G. This was evaluated by ICP-OES, and the Ca and P losses were compared between the CPP-ACP-coated and control groups. Ca and P losses from CPP-ACP-, GO@CPP-ACP-, and GQDs@CPP-ACP coated enamel were significantly lower than those from the control group (P < 0.05). However, the GO-and GQDs coated enamel did not show a significant reduction compared to the control group (P > 0.05). CPP-ACP was demonstrated to inhibit demineralisation when combined with GO and GQDs; however, GO and GQDs alone did not show any anti-demineralisation ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Artificial saliva/biofilm cycling in a biofilm-challenged environment
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 A displays SEM image of the artificial caries lesion. The mean (± SD) lesion depth was 137.4 ± 13.01 μm. No significant differences were observed among the groups. To avoid the possible intervention of teeth variation, the samples of different groups were chosen randomly. Fig. 6B shows the biofilm structure after 5 days. Fig. 6C shows representative reconstructed micro-CT images of the six groups. The lesion depth was tested before the treatment, and no significant differences were found among different tooth slides.

Enamel slices with GO@CPP-ACP showed the least number of carious lesions, and the lesions were even shallower than before. The lesion depths of GO, GQDs, and GQDs@CPP-ACP did not change significantly compared to those at the starting point, whereas the CPP-ACP and control groups had significantly deeper carious lesions than before. Fig. 6F indicates that GO@CPP-ACP and GQDs@CPP-ACP had significant effects on the enamel mineral content.

The pH of BHIS after incubation for 4 h was tested daily, as shown in Fig. 6D. For the CPP-ACP and control groups, the pH decreased to approximately 4.4–4.5 each day. In contrast, for the GO, GQDs, GO@CPP-ACP, and GQDs@CPP-ACP groups, the pH was close to 7. During the 7-day cycle, the pH values of BHIS after 4-hour immersion, the pH of the CPP-ACP and control groups were significantly lower than those of the groups containing GO and GQDs (P < 0.05). Although GO showed better biofilm inhibition ability than the GQDs, no significant difference was observed in the pH each day. This may be related to the GO and GQDs concentrations used.

CCK8 assay was performed to detect the proliferative activity of HGF-1. As shown in Fig. 7 , after culturing with GO or GQDs for 4 h, 3 days, and 7 days, the proliferation activity increased in the GO group, but decreased in the GQDs group, especially at 7 d. There was no significant difference between GO and GQDs at different chemical concentrations (P > 0.05) after 4 h and 3 days. However, after 7 days of incubation, the GQDs showed significant differences at 4×MIC and 8×MIC. This may be due to the higher concentration of GQDs compared with that of GO.

This is the first study to use GO and GQDs to improve the anticaries effect of CPP-ACP by inhibiting biofilm growth and promoting remineralisation. The results showed that GO and GQDs can inhibit the growth of S. mutans biofilms, promote CPP-ACP remineralisation in cyclic artificial saliva/biofilm environments, and have an ideal biocompatible effect. GO exhibited a better effect than GQDs with a lower dose and better anticaries performance.

S. mutans is considered the main microorganism causing dental caries. Inhibiting S. mutans colonisation and biofilm formation would decrease the risk to the oral cavity by producing less acid to attack the dental hard tissue [19]. Our study demonstrates that GO and GQDs can resist bacterial growth and biofilm formation. The anti-biofouling effects of GO and GQDs were demonstrated by analysing the CFU values, SEM, and confocal microscopy. The biofilms on the GO- and GQD-coated enamel slices showed a strong decrease in bacterial growth for all tested groups after 24 h. GQDs had a biofilm inhibitory effect at a higher dose than GO. Recent studies reported the biofilm inhibition synergism effects of GQDs at 100 μg/mL with 10 mM H2O2 for S. aureus at 2.5 h incubation [20], and GO was shown cutting E. coli membrane under 125 μg/mL concentration [21]. SEM and confocal microscopy results indicated that for enamel slides coated with GO and GQDs, very little biofilm was observed after 24 h of incubation. However, extracellular biofilms were present in both the CPP-ACP and control groups. GQDs have been widely discussed for improving the antibiofilm activity against S. aureus biofilms at low doses by increasing the production of reactive oxygen species [22]. The mechanisms underlying the antimicrobial activity of GO may differ. Some studies have reported that the mechanism of GO might rely on its sharp edges and generate oxidative stress, which leads to membrane destabilisation, leakage of cytoplasmic fluid, and cell death [23].

Our study demonstrated the remineralisation effect of CPP-ACP containing GO or GQDs on human tooth enamel, showing a statistically significant difference between the experimental and study groups. The mean ICP and Ca/P ratios of the CPP-ACP-treated groups were significantly higher than those of the control group and groups containing GO or GQDs only. However, the differences between the mean ICP and Ca/P ratio in the GO, GQD, and control groups were not significant. In the SEM analysis, a honeycomb pattern was evident in the GO, GQD, and control groups after 1-day remineralization. Interestingly, although hydroxyapatite crystals were uniformly deposited on the enamel prisms, the groups containing GQDs@CPP-ACP and GO@CPP-ACP exhibited needle-like crystal deposition patterns. Although combinations of CPP-ACP succeeded in growing calcium-rich crystal bundles, this approach did not result in enamel-like biomaterials in terms of structure. Based on these results, GO@CPP-ACP and GQDs@CPP-ACP tended to replace the lost demineralised tissue of the lesion with remineralised material. Studies have shown that GO can be fabricated using enamel-like hydroxyapatite crystals with a highly organised preferential orientation along the c-axis [24]. GO at 1.0 wt% inhibited Staphylococcus aureus and formed needle-like crystals with high purity after 10 mA/10 h of electrophoresis [25].

In this study, we investigated the effects of various treatments on dental samples, and it is important to consider the potential influence of inherent teeth variation on our results. Although measures were taken to ensure sample consistency, such as random selection and pre-experiment characterization, natural variability in the teeth could still impact the outcomes. The Ca/P ratio and lesion depth were measured before the experiments, showing no significant differences, which suggests uniformity in mineral composition. Additionally, SEM analysis confirmed consistent structural integrity and surface morphology across samples before treatment. These steps were aimed at minimizing the impact of variation; however, subtle differences in enamel and dentin properties, such as microstructure or organic content, could still affect treatment efficacy. Future studies could benefit from a more detailed characterization of tooth properties, potentially including hardness testing or more advanced imaging techniques, to understand and control these variables. Moreover, increasing the sample size could help mitigate the impact of outliers and provide a more robust dataset.

The processes of demineralisation and remineralisation are balanced in the oral environment, and carious lesions are formed by the breakdown of this balance [26]. Caries bacteria such as S. mutans, accumulate on the enamel biofilm and form organic acids, which lower the pH of the local microenvironment and initiate demineralisation. If the balance was changed, demineralisation would dominate, and caries would begin [27]. An artificial saliva/biofilm cycling model was designed to mimic the dynamics of mineral loss and gain involved in caries formation. Although the chemical model is time-saving and inexpensive, this pH-cycling model could mimic a carious lesion in a complex oral environment and might be more sensitive than the chemical pH-cycling model for use in clinical situations. No study has concluded that GO and GQDs can promote the anticaries effect of enamel in a cyclic artificial saliva/biofilm environment. Some studies highlighted the important changes in biofilms but did not explore the improvement of the anticaries effect in a biofilm-challenged environment [28]. Thus, an artificial saliva/biofilm cycle was developed, which is different from that reported in previous studies. According to the micro-CT results, demineralisation occurred in the control and CPP-ACP groups compared with the baseline after artificial saliva/biofilm cycling, whereas the GO and GQDs groups alone only showed light mineral loss. GO@CPP-ACP and GQDs@CPP-ACP displayed significant mineral increases, which may account for their high pH values. The pH of BHIS after 4 h of immersion in GO- or GQD-containing groups was approximately 6.5. This artificial saliva/biofilm cycle refers to food cycling and biofilm reformation during remineralisation in an artificial saliva solution. Our results indicate that CPP-ACP combined with GO and GQDs was more resistant to subsequent acid challenges.

The toxic effect of GO and GQDs has been shown to be dependent on dose and time of exposure. No highly toxic effects were found in this study based on human cell cultures according to the CCK8 assay. Other studies have shown that GO and GQDs are highly biocompatible and have low toxicity, allowing their use as supports for tissue regeneration and cell growth [29]. The toxic effect of GO was not observed at 4 h, 3 days, or 7 days, which was confirmed by the CCK8 levels. However, for GQDs, toxicity increased with dose, concentration, and exposure duration. This difference between GO and GQDs may have been caused by the different MIC concentrations. Other studies have reached the same conclusion that GO is highly biocompatible and shows low toxicity levels, thus allowing it to be used as a support for tissue regeneration and cell growth [30]. Numerous other studies have reached similar conclusions [31].

Several biofilm inhibition agents and antibiotics have been used to improve the bactericidal efficiency of CPP-ACP, which results in low cytotoxicity under physiological conditions and helps to decrease the caries rate [32]. In this regard, caries-related bacterial biofilm inhibition and remineralisation promotion may be suitable approaches for preventing dental caries. An anti-S. mutans membrane combining lysozyme nanofilm and polyphemusin I on the enamel surface showed higher activity in inhibiting demineralisation [33]. An antibacterial-functionalized enamel-like structure of fluorapatite has been proven to kill S. mutans, which has the advantage of resisting secondary caries caused by cariogenic bacteria [34]. It has been reported that an enamel-inspired macroscopic bioactive material with a GO-chitosan matrix possesses antibacterial adhesion properties, and more Ca ions combine with the active sites of the crystallite [35].

Our results suggest that applying GO or GQDs based on CPP-ACP could be an effective biofilm inhibition method, particularly for treating caries caused by S. mutans. The development of biocompatible antimicrobial materials could increase the success rate of caries prevention. It is imperative to explore the biofilm inhibition and antibiofilm effects of nanohybrids and develop a cost-effective and safe treatment to minimise the risks associated with caries. Our study found that GO and GQDs have the ability to inhibit biofilms and antibiofilm effects and could enhance the anticaries effect in a biofilm-challenged environment. Enamel coated with GO@CPP-ACP or GQDs@CPP-ACP showed an antibiofilm effect on S. mutans and improved remineralisation ability compared to the control and CPP-ACP groups. To highlight this, the remineralisation and anti-biofouling abilities of CPP-ACP were improved. The biofilm inhibition and antibiofilm properties of GO and GQDs combined with CPP-ACP on S. mutans were most significant for GO, followed by GQDs. In the low-pH environment that we created, GO and GQDs improved the remineralisation of CPP-ACP by maintaining a pH near 7. Moreover, it did not show any obvious toxicity in cells. GO exhibited better biofilm inhibition at lower doses and did not cause any adverse effects.

This study provided valuable insights into the composition and surface characteristics of enamel hydroxyapatite crystals using EDX, Ca/P ratio measurements, and SEM. However, a notable limitation is the absence of X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis, which is a standard method for determining crystal structures. Due to equipment limitations, we were unable to perform XRD analysis. While EDX confirmed the elemental composition and the Ca/P ratio measurements supported the expected stoichiometry of hydroxyapatite, these methods do not provide detailed information of enamel structure. SEM imaging offered insights into the surface morphology but also lacks the ability to fully characterize the crystal structure. Future research should include XRD analysis to provide a comprehensive characterization of the crystal structure and validate the compositional and morphological findings obtained in this study. By addressing these limitations, subsequent studies can enhance the understanding of hydroxyapatite's structural properties and its implications for the research. Despite limitations, our study successfully demonstrated the enhanced anti-caries effects of GO@CPP-ACP and GQDs@CPP-ACP in a biofilm-challenged environment. The results indicated that the interventions were effective in preventing caries, underscoring their potential application in dental care.

This study aimed to compare the anticaries effect of GO and GQDs with that of a commonly used remineralisation agent. The enamel slices covered by GO and GQDs with CPP-ACP showed good ability for apatite structure modification and biofilm inhibition and were more resistant to acidic environments, which supports the hypothesis that the use of GO and GQDs improves CPP-ACP to prevent caries-related demineralisation. These results encourage further in vivo investigations of GO or GQDs combined with CPP-ACP materials to improve their remineralisation ability.

Cheng Lu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Yu Yuan Zhang: Resources, Methodology, Data curation. Si-min Peng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Min Gu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Hai Ming Wong: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: All authors declared that they have no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by a grant from the Research Grants Council of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region, China (Project No. 17102921). The funding recipient was Hai Ming Wong.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jdent.2024.105319.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An enamel-inspired bioactive material with multiscale structure and antibacterial adhesion property
